document_title: The Adventure of the Three Students
document_summary: Sherlock Holmes investigates a scandal involving an attempted cheating scheme on a prestigious scholarship examination at St. Luke's College. Through deductive reasoning and physical evidence found at the scene—including specialized clay and pencil shavings—Holmes identifies the culprit as a student named Gilchrist, whose actions were partially shielded by a loyal family servant, Bannister.

chunks.title: Introduction to the Case
chunks.chunk:
THE ADVENTURE OF THE THREE STUDENTS
 Arthur Conan Doyle
chunks.summary: Title and authorship of the short story.

chunks.title: The Setting and Premises
chunks.chunk:
It was in the year '95 that a combination of events, into which I
 need not enter, caused Mr. Sherlock Holmes and myself to spend some
 weeks in one of our great University towns, and it was during this
 time that the small but instructive adventure which I am about to
 relate befell us...
chunks.summary: Watson introduces the context of a scandal at a university town in 1895, promising discretion to protect the reputation of those involved.
questions_raised: What is the nature of the scandal?

chunks.title: The Arrival of Mr. Soames
chunks.chunk:
We were residing at the time in furnished lodgings close to a library
 where Sherlock Holmes was pursuing some laborious researches in early
 English charters... Here it was that one evening we received a visit from an acquaintance, Mr. Hilton Soames, tutor and lecturer at the College of St. Luke's.
chunks.summary: Holmes and Watson are interrupted by Hilton Soames, a distressed tutor from St. Luke's College.

chunks.title: Soames's Request
chunks.chunk:
"I trust, Mr. Holmes, that you can spare me a few hours of your
 valuable time. We have had a very painful incident at St. Luke's..."
chunks.summary: Soames asks Holmes for help with a sensitive incident at his college, hoping to avoid police involvement.
action_items: Help resolve a sensitive scandal at the college.

chunks.title: Maintaining Discretion
chunks.chunk:
"No, no, my dear sir; such a course is utterly impossible. When once
 the law is evoked it cannot be stayed again..."
chunks.summary: Soames emphasizes the necessity of discretion to protect the college's reputation.

chunks.title: Holmes Accepts
chunks.chunk:
My friend's temper had not improved since he had been deprived of the
 congenial surroundings of Baker Street... He shrugged his shoulders in ungracious acquiescence...
chunks.summary: Holmes begrudgingly agrees to hear the story.

chunks.title: The Fortescue Scholarship
chunks.chunk:
"I must explain to you, Mr. Holmes, that to-morrow is the first day
 of the examination for the Fortescue Scholarship..."
chunks.summary: Soames explains that as an examiner for the prestigious Fortescue Scholarship, he is responsible for keeping the secret Greek translation paper safe.

chunks.title: Leaving the Proofs
chunks.chunk:
"To-day about three o'clock the proofs of this paper arrived from the
 printers... At four-thirty my task was not yet completed. I had,
 however, promised to take tea... so I left the proof upon my desk."
chunks.summary: Soames describes leaving the exam proofs unattended for over an hour.

chunks.title: The Key in the Door
chunks.chunk:
"You are aware, Mr. Holmes, that our college doors are double... As I approached
 my outer door I was amazed to see a key in it... The only duplicate... was that which belonged to my servant, Bannister..."
chunks.summary: Soames discovers his servant Bannister's key left in the lock, implying someone might have entered the room while he was away.

chunks.title: Evidence of Disturbance
chunks.chunk:
"The moment I looked at my table I was aware that someone had
 rummaged among my papers... I found that one of them was lying on
 the floor, one was on the side table..."
chunks.summary: Soames notices the exam papers were moved and tampered with.

chunks.title: Holmes's Observation
chunks.chunk:
Holmes stirred for the first time. "The first page on the floor, the second in the window, the third
 where you left it," said he.
chunks.summary: Holmes deduces the exact location of the disturbed papers, showing immediate insight.

chunks.title: Soames's Surprise
chunks.chunk:
"Exactly, Mr. Holmes. You amaze me. How could you possibly know
 that?"
chunks.summary: Soames is astonished by Holmes's accuracy.

chunks.title: Suspecting Intrusion
chunks.chunk:
"For an instant I imagined that Bannister had taken the unpardonable
 liberty... The alternative was that someone passing had observed the key in the door..."
chunks.summary: Soames considers the possibility of an outside intruder looking to gain an advantage in the scholarship.
questions_raised: Who had the opportunity to enter?

chunks.title: Physical Traces
chunks.chunk:
"Bannister was very much upset... I soon saw that the
 intruder had left other traces... On the table in the window were several shreds from a pencil... A broken tip of lead..."
chunks.summary: Soames reports finding physical evidence: pencil shavings and a broken lead, suggesting the culprit copied the papers.

chunks.title: Holmes's Engagement
chunks.chunk:
"Excellent!" said Holmes, who was recovering his good-humour as his
 attention became more engrossed by the case. "Fortune has been your
 friend."
chunks.summary: Holmes becomes interested in the case due to the evidence.

chunks.title: Additional Evidence
chunks.chunk:
"This was not all. I have a new writing-table... I found a clean cut in it... on the table I found a small ball of black dough, or clay, with
 specks of something which looks like sawdust in it."
chunks.summary: Soames reports more physical evidence: a cut in the desk and a mysterious ball of clay with sawdust.
questions_raised: Where did the clay come from?

chunks.title: Potential Suspects
chunks.chunk:
"I shall be happy to look into it... Had anyone visited you in your room after the papers came to you?"
 "Yes; young Daulat Ras, an Indian student..."
chunks.summary: Holmes asks if anyone had access, identifying a student named Daulat Ras who visited Soames earlier.

chunks.title: Investigating Access
chunks.chunk: "But might be recognised as proofs?"... "Did anyone know that these proofs would be there?"... "No one save the printer."
chunks.summary: Holmes probes whether others knew about the presence of the sensitive papers.

chunks.title: Heading to the Scene
chunks.chunk:
"You left your door open?"
 "I locked up the papers first."
 "Then it amounts to this... the man who tampered with them came upon them accidentally..."
chunks.summary: Holmes concludes that the intruder likely found the papers accidentally and prepares to visit the scene.

chunks.title: Observing the Courtyard
chunks.chunk: The sitting-room of our client opened... on to the ancient lichen-tinted court... Holmes halted and looked earnestly at the window.
chunks.summary: Holmes examines the exterior layout of the college rooms.
questions_raised: What is Holmes looking for at the window?

chunks.title: Examining the Room
chunks.chunk: "He must have entered through the door..."... We stood at the entrance while Holmes made an examination of the carpet.
chunks.summary: Holmes begins a physical inspection of the room.

chunks.title: Analyzing the Servant's Chair
chunks.chunk:
"I am afraid there are no signs here," said he. "One could hardly
 hope for any upon so dry a day. Your servant seems to have quite
 recovered. You left him in a chair, you say; which chair?"
chunks.summary: Holmes focuses on the location of Bannister the servant following the discovery.
questions_raised: Why is Holmes interested in the chair?

chunks.title: Reconstructing the Crime
chunks.chunk: "I see. Near this little table... The man entered and took the papers... He carried them over to the window table..."
chunks.summary: Holmes reconstructs the thief's actions in the room.

chunks.title: Intruder's Escape
chunks.chunk:
"As a matter of fact he could not," said Soames, "for I entered by
 the side door."
chunks.summary: Soames clarifies that the side door prevented a direct escape.

chunks.title: Timeline of Theft
chunks.chunk:
"Ah, that's good! Well, anyhow, that was in his mind. Let me see the
 three strips... How long would it take him to do that... A quarter of an hour, not less."
chunks.summary: Holmes estimates the time taken to copy the papers as at least fifteen minutes.

chunks.title: No Noise
chunks.chunk:
"You were not aware of any hurrying feet
 on the stair as you entered the outer door?"
 "No, I can't say I was."
chunks.summary: Holmes questions if Soames heard any movement, noting the lack of sound.

chunks.title: The Specific Pencil
chunks.chunk:
"Well, he wrote so furiously that he broke his pencil... The
 pencil was not an ordinary one. It was above the usual size, with a
 soft lead; the outer colour was dark blue..."
chunks.summary: Holmes describes the specific features of the pencil left behind, providing a key lead.

chunks.title: The Pencil Clue
chunks.chunk: Mr. Soames was somewhat overwhelmed by this flood of information... Holmes held out a small chip with the letters NN...
chunks.summary: Holmes shows Soames a chip of the pencil containing the letters 'NN', pointing toward a specific brand.

chunks.title: Johann Faber
chunks.chunk:
"Watson, I have always done you an injustice... You are aware that
 Johann Faber is the most common maker's name... And the cut--a positive tear, I
 see."
chunks.summary: Holmes connects the 'NN' to the brand 'Johann Faber' and examines the table for further impressions.

chunks.title: Examining the Bedroom
chunks.chunk:
"To my bedroom."
 "Have you been in it since your adventure?"
 "No; I came straight away for you."
 "I should like to have a glance round."
chunks.summary: Holmes decides to search Soames's bedroom for additional evidence.
questions_raised: What will he find in the bedroom?

chunks.title: A Discovery in the Bedroom
chunks.chunk: As Holmes drew the curtain... Holmes turned away and stooped suddenly to the floor. "Halloa! What's this?" said he.
chunks.summary: Holmes finds something suspicious in the bedroom.

chunks.title: Matching Clay
chunks.chunk:
"It was a small pyramid of black, putty-like stuff, exactly like the
 one upon the table... 'Your visitor seems to have left traces in your bedroom as well as in
 your sitting-room, Mr. Soames.'"
chunks.summary: Holmes identifies another lump of clay in the bedroom, identical to the one in the sitting room.

chunks.title: A Hiding Culprit
chunks.chunk:
"I think it is clear enough. You came back by an unexpected way... He caught up everything which would betray him and he rushed into
 your bedroom to conceal himself."
chunks.summary: Holmes deduces that the intruder hid in the bedroom while Soames was talking to Bannister.

chunks.title: Alternative Escape
chunks.chunk:
"Surely there is another alternative... I don't know
 whether you observed my bedroom window?"... "The man might have effected his entrance there..."
chunks.summary: Soames suggests the intruder might have entered or escaped through the bedroom window.

chunks.title: The Three Students
chunks.chunk:
Holmes shook his head... "I understand you to say that there
 are three students who use this stair... And they are all in for this examination?"
chunks.summary: Holmes asks about the three students living on the same staircase who are taking the exam.

chunks.title: Suspicions
chunks.chunk: "Let us hear the suspicions. I will look after the proofs."
chunks.summary: Holmes demands to hear Soames's suspicions about the three students.

chunks.title: Gilchrist
chunks.chunk:
"The lower of the three is Gilchrist, a fine
 scholar and athlete... His father was the notorious Sir Jabez
 Gilchrist..."
chunks.summary: Soames describes Gilchrist, a talented athlete from a fallen, impoverished family.

chunks.title: Daulat Ras
chunks.chunk:
"The second floor is inhabited by Daulat Ras, the Indian... He is a
 quiet, inscrutable fellow... steady and methodical."
chunks.summary: Soames describes Daulat Ras as a steady, methodical Indian student.

chunks.title: Miles McLaren
chunks.chunk:
"The top floor belongs to Miles McLaren. He is a brilliant fellow... wayward, dissipated, and unprincipled... I dare not go so far as that. But of the three he is perhaps the
 least unlikely."
chunks.summary: Soames describes Miles McLaren as a brilliant but troubled and unprincipled student, suggesting he is a prime suspect.

chunks.title: Questioning Bannister
chunks.chunk:
"Exactly. Now, Mr. Soames, let us have a look at your servant,
 Bannister."
 ... "I understand," said Holmes, "that you left your key in the door?"
chunks.summary: Holmes begins questioning the servant, Bannister, about the key.

chunks.title: The Servant's Account
chunks.chunk:
"It was most unfortunate, sir. But I have occasionally done the same
 thing at other times."
 ... "I had the tea-tray in my hand. I thought I would come back for the
 key. Then I forgot."
chunks.summary: Bannister explains that he forgot the key accidentally while carrying a tea tray.

chunks.title: Bannister's Nervousness
chunks.chunk:
"Yes, sir. Such a thing has never happened... I nearly fainted, sir."
 "So I understand. Where were you when you began to feel bad?"
 "Where was I, sir? Why, here, near the door."
chunks.summary: Holmes questions Bannister about where he sat when he felt ill, noting an inconsistency in his behavior.
questions_raised: Why did he sit near the door instead of other chairs?

chunks.title: Bannister's Loyalty
chunks.chunk:
"I really don't think he knew much about it... I don't believe there is any
 gentleman in this University who is capable of profiting by such an
 action."
chunks.summary: Bannister expresses disbelief that any student would cheat.

chunks.title: Preparing for the Night
chunks.chunk:
"Thank you; that will do," said Holmes... "Now, Mr. Soames, we will take a walk in the quadrangle, if you please."
 Three yellow squares of light shone above us in the gathering gloom.
chunks.summary: Holmes finishes questioning Bannister and takes Soames outside to watch the students' rooms.
questions_raised: Are the students in their rooms?

chunks.title: Watching the Students
chunks.chunk:
"Your three birds are all in their nests," said Holmes... It was the Indian, whose dark silhouette appeared suddenly upon his
 blind.
chunks.summary: Holmes observes that all three students are in their rooms.
questions_raised: What is the Indian student doing?

chunks.title: Inspecting the Rooms
chunks.chunk: "No difficulty in the world," Soames answered... "No names, please!" said Holmes, as we knocked at Gilchrist's door.
chunks.summary: Soames arranges for Holmes to visit the rooms of the three students under the guise of an architectural tour.

chunks.title: Visits to Students
chunks.chunk:
Holmes was so charmed with one of them that he insisted on drawing it... The same curious accident happened to him
 in the rooms of the Indian... Only at the third did our visit prove abortive.
chunks.summary: Holmes visits the rooms of Gilchrist and Daulat Ras, but McLaren refuses entry, shouting abuse.

chunks.title: Height Inquiry
chunks.chunk: "A rude fellow," said our guide... Holmes's response was a curious one. "Can you tell me his exact height?" he asked.
chunks.summary: Soames complains about McLaren, while Holmes focuses on the student's height.

chunks.title: Holmes's Departure
chunks.chunk:
"That is very important," said Holmes. "And now, Mr. Soames, I wish
 you good-night."
chunks.summary: Holmes leaves Soames for the night, promising to solve the issue the next morning.

chunks.title: Instructions for Soames
chunks.chunk:
"You must leave it as it is. I shall drop round early to-morrow
 morning... You can be perfectly easy in your mind."
chunks.summary: Holmes tells Soames not to take any action until he returns the next morning.
action_items: Do not change anything until morning.

chunks.title: Reflecting on the Clues
chunks.chunk:
When we were out in the darkness... "Well, Watson, what do you think of it?"... "Quite a little parlour game--sort of
 three-card trick, is it not?"
chunks.summary: Holmes and Watson discuss the puzzle, reflecting on the three students.

chunks.title: Watson's Suspects
chunks.chunk:
"The foul-mouthed fellow at the top. He is the one with the worst
 record. And yet that Indian was a sly fellow also."
chunks.summary: Watson shares his thoughts on the suspects: McLaren and Daulat Ras.

chunks.title: Holmes's Uncertainty
chunks.chunk:
"But that fellow does puzzle me."
 "Who?"
 "Why, Bannister, the servant. What's his game in the matter?"
chunks.summary: Holmes expresses confusion regarding the servant, Bannister.

chunks.title: Searching for Pencil Shops
chunks.chunk: "So he did me. That's the puzzling part... well, well, here's a large stationer's. We shall begin our researches here."
chunks.summary: Holmes and Watson decide to search for the specialized pencil in local stationer shops.

chunks.title: Stationer Research
chunks.chunk:
There were only four stationers of any consequence in the town, and
 at each Holmes produced his pencil chips... All were agreed that one could be ordered, but that it was
 not a usual size...
chunks.summary: They visit local shops but are unable to find a match for the pencil, confirming it is rare.

chunks.title: Holmes's Confidence
chunks.chunk:
"No good, my dear Watson. This, the best and only final clue, has run
 to nothing. But, indeed, I have little doubt that we can build up a
 sufficient case without it."
chunks.summary: Holmes isn't discouraged, believing they have enough evidence to build the case.

chunks.title: Next Morning
chunks.chunk:
Holmes made no further allusion... At
 eight in the morning he came into my room... "Well, Watson," said he, "it is time we went down to St. Luke's."
chunks.summary: Holmes and Watson return to St. Luke's the next morning.

chunks.title: Solved Mystery
chunks.chunk:
"You have formed a conclusion?"
 "Yes, my dear Watson; I have solved the mystery."
 "But what fresh evidence could you have got?"
chunks.summary: Holmes confirms that he has solved the mystery.

chunks.title: New Evidence
chunks.chunk:
Aha! It is not for nothing that I have turned myself out of bed at
 the untimely hour of six. I have put in two hours' hard work... He held out his hand. On the palm were three little pyramids of
 black, doughy clay.
chunks.summary: Holmes reveals he found another piece of the black clay, confirming the source.

chunks.title: Facing the Tutor
chunks.chunk:
The unfortunate tutor was certainly in a state of pitiable agitation
 when we found him in his chambers. ... "Shall the examination proceed?"
 "Yes; let it proceed by all means."
chunks.summary: They find Soames, who is anxious about the upcoming exam.

chunks.title: A Private Court-Martial
chunks.chunk:
"He shall not compete."
 "You know him?"
 "I think so. If this matter is not to become public we must give
 ourselves certain powers..."
chunks.summary: Holmes organizes a private confrontation in the room.

chunks.title: Bannister Confronted
chunks.chunk:
Bannister entered, and shrunk back in evident surprise and fear... "Now, Bannister, will
 you please tell us the truth about yesterday's incident?"
chunks.summary: Holmes confronts Bannister, pressing him to admit his role.
action_items: Tell the truth about the incident.

chunks.title: Accusing Bannister
chunks.chunk:
"But it seems probable enough, since the moment that Mr. Soames's back was turned you released the man who was
 hiding in that bedroom."
chunks.summary: Holmes accuses Bannister of helping the intruder escape.

chunks.title: Summoning Gilchrist
chunks.chunk:
"In that case you can give us no further information... Now, Soames, I am going to ask you to have the great kindness to go
 up to the room of young Gilchrist..."
chunks.summary: Holmes calls for Gilchrist to come to the room, revealing he is the prime suspect.

chunks.title: Confronting Gilchrist
chunks.chunk:
An instant later the tutor returned, bringing with him the student... "We want to know, Mr. Gilchrist, how you, an honourable man, ever came to commit
 such an action as that of yesterday?"
chunks.summary: Gilchrist enters and is confronted with his actions.

chunks.title: Gilchrist's Confession
chunks.chunk:
The next he had thrown himself on his knees beside
 the table and, burying his face in his hands, he had burst into a
 storm of passionate sobbing.
chunks.summary: Gilchrist collapses and breaks down in tears.

chunks.title: Holmes's Summary
chunks.chunk:
"Come, come," said Holmes, kindly... "Perhaps it would
 be easier for you if I were to tell Mr. Soames what occurred..."
chunks.summary: Holmes offers to summarize the events to spare Gilchrist the pain of explaining them.

chunks.title: Deducing the Intruder
chunks.chunk: "From the moment, Mr. Soames, that you said to me that no one... could have told that the papers were in your room... The man who entered knew that the papers were there."
chunks.summary: Holmes explains his reasoning, starting with how the culprit knew the papers were present.

chunks.title: The Height Clue
chunks.chunk:
"When I approached your room I measured how tall
 a man would need to be in order to see as he passed what papers were
 on the central table. I am six feet high... No one less than that would have a chance."
chunks.summary: Holmes explains that he measured the height required to see the papers from the window, which implicated the taller students.

chunks.title: The Jumping Connection
chunks.chunk:
"I could make nothing [of the centre table], until in
 your description of Gilchrist you mentioned that he was a
 long-distance jumper. Then the whole thing came to me in an instant..."
chunks.summary: Holmes realizes that Gilchrist's identity as a long-distance jumper explains the physical traces left on the desk.

chunks.title: Gilchrist's Impulse
chunks.chunk:
"What happened was this. This young fellow had employed his afternoon
 at the athletic grounds... As he passed your window he saw... these proofs... A sudden impulse came over
 him to enter..."
chunks.summary: Holmes explains that Gilchrist acted on impulse after seeing the papers while holding his spiked jumping shoes.

chunks.title: The Evidence Explained
chunks.chunk:
"He thought the tutor must return by the main gate... Suddenly he heard him
 at the very door... He forgot his gloves... I walked out to the athletic grounds... saw that tenacious black clay..."
chunks.summary: Holmes links the gloves, the clay, and the spikes to Gilchrist's shoes, solving the case.

chunks.title: The Culprit Admits
chunks.chunk: The student had drawn himself erect. "Yes, sir, it is true," said he. "Good heavens, have you nothing to add?" cried Soames.
chunks.summary: Gilchrist admits the truth.
questions_raised: What else does Gilchrist have to add?

chunks.title: A Change of Plan
chunks.chunk:
"Yes, sir, I have... I have a letter here... I have determined not to go in for the examination. I
 have been offered a commission in the Rhodesian Police..."
chunks.summary: Gilchrist reveals he had already decided not to take the exam and is instead joining the Rhodesian Police.

chunks.title: Bannister's Role
chunks.chunk:
"I am indeed pleased... But why did you change your
 purpose?"
 Gilchrist pointed to Bannister. "There is the man who set me in the right path."
chunks.summary: Gilchrist credits Bannister with guiding him toward the correct moral path.

chunks.title: Bannister's Motivation
chunks.chunk:
"It was simple enough... I was butler to old Sir Jabez Gilchrist... When he was ruined I came to the college... I
 watched his son all I could for the sake of the old days."
chunks.summary: Bannister explains his deep, long-standing loyalty to Gilchrist's family, which motivated him to protect the young man from his mistake.

chunks.title: Resolution
chunks.chunk:
"No, indeed," said Holmes, heartily... "Well,
 Soames, I think we have cleared your little problem up... I trust
 that a bright future awaits you in Rhodesia."
chunks.summary: Holmes concludes the case, expresses approval of the resolution, and departs with Watson.

chunks.title: Disclaimer
chunks.chunk:
----------
 This text is provided to you "as-is" without any warranty...
chunks.summary: Standard text document disclaimer.
